figures.caption: Fig. 2. The architecture of PIDNet. RGB, optical flow, and audio features are extracted by pretrained backbones and projected into a shared latent space, with a zero-initialized fused feature serving as the initial cross-modal representation. The features are then progressively refined through three fusion stages, where iMambaWave performs global and fine-grained decoupling while enhancing temporal context modeling. Each stage contains a Group3M Block, in which MoCA selects complementary information to enrich the fused features and MKConv captures multi-scale local patterns. The final action quality score is predicted by global average pooling and a fully connected layer.

Fig. 4. Visualization of intermediate feature response maps across the three progressive fusion stages for a representative test sample from the ball category of the RG dataset.

TABLE VI ABLATION STUDIES ON DIFFERENT COMPONENT COMBINATIONS OF PROPOSED IMAMBAWAVE IN THE FIS-V DATASET.

TABLE I COMPARISON WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART APPROACHES IS MEASURED BY SPEARMAN’S RANK CORRELATION ρ (THE HIGHER THE BETTER) AND MSE (THE LOWER THE BETTER) ON THE RHYTHMIC GYMNASTICS DATASET. FISHER’S Z-VALUE IS USED TO COMPUTE THE AVERAGE ρ ACROSS ACTIONS. BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD AND UNDERLINED.
TABLE II COMPARISON WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART APPROACHES IS MEASURED BY SPEARMAN’S RANK CORRELATION ρ AND MSE ON FIS-V DATASET.

Fig. 1. Illustration of the iMambaWave module. The input feature F (l) m is first split along the channel dimension to decouple it into an identity branch and an enhancement branch. The identity branch preserves modality-specific discriminative information, while the enhancement branch is processed by a Bi-Mamba path and a wavelet-transform path to capture long-range temporal dependencies and local frequency variations, respectively. A learned gate σ(l) adaptively fuses the two enhanced features, which are then concatenated with the identity feature to obtain the output F̃(l) m .

Fig. 5. Qualitative visualization of test samples along the multimodal temporal dimension. The first sample is clubs #001 from the RG dataset, and the second is video #478 from the PCS class in the Fis-V dataset. The top row presents representative key frames, the middle row shows the audio corresponding to each test sample, and the bottom row depicts the normalized saliency curves of the RGB, optical flow, audio, and fused modalities. Higher curve values indicate a greater contribution of the corresponding time step to the final score.

Fig. 6. Qualitative visualization of the iMambaWave gate responses for the test sample ball #033 from RG dataset. The top row shows representative frames aligned with the marked positions a–d. In the bottom row, (a)-(c) present the fusion gate curves of the Group3M block at stage 1-3, respectively, while (d) shows the intra-branch iMambaWave gate responses of RGB, optical flow, and audio before entering the Group3M block.
